title: Your First Ryokan: What to Expect, What to Do, and What Not to Wear
excerpt: A traditional Japanese inn is not a hotel. The sooner you accept that, the better the stay.
body: The check-in time at a ryokan is usually 3pm and checkout is 10am. These aren't suggestions. Your room is being transformed twice a day — futon laid out at turndown, rolled away before breakfast — and that choreography is coordinated with dinner and bath times. Arriving at 6pm and expecting your room to be ready the way a hotel room is always ready will result in confusion on both sides.

When you arrive, you remove your shoes at the entrance and switch into slippers. The slippers go everywhere except tatami rooms — in tatami rooms you go barefoot or in socks. There will be a separate pair of toilet slippers in the bathroom. Put them on to enter, take them off to leave. Forgetting and wandering the hallway in toilet slippers is the ryokan equivalent of walking through a dinner party in a life jacket.

Your room will have: a low table, zabuton cushions, a tokonoma (a small alcove, usually with a seasonal arrangement), a yukata folded somewhere, and a kettle with tea. The yukata is your uniform. Wear it to the onsen, to dinner if the place is traditional, back to your room. It's a cotton robe, not a costume. Left side over right — right over left is how the dead are dressed.

The onsen, if there is one, operates on a schedule — typically gender-segregated, sometimes with rotating access to different baths. The rules: wash thoroughly at the shower station before entering the communal water. Don't bring a towel into the bath. The small hand towel goes on your head or beside the tub. Don't get in if you've been drinking heavily — people faint, and it's embarrassing for everyone.

Dinner is kaiseki — a multi-course meal built around seasonal ingredients. It arrives in a sequence you don't choose and can't rush. Don't ask for the menu in advance. Don't request variations unless you have a genuine allergy. The meal takes 90 minutes to two hours, the plates will be small, and you will feel, briefly, like you're not getting enough food. You are getting enough food. The point is rhythm.

Breakfast follows the same logic: rice, miso soup, pickled vegetables, grilled fish, tofu, a small egg dish. It arrives at a fixed time, usually between 7:30 and 9am. It is also the best breakfast in the world, and I say this as someone who is not generally enthusiastic about breakfast.

Prices run from around ¥8,000 per person at a decent mid-range option with onsen and breakfast to ¥80,000+ per person at the higher end with full kaiseki. The sweet spot is in the ¥15,000–¥25,000 range — particularly in onsen towns like Kinosaki Onsen in Hyogo or Gero in Gifu, which don't have the premium of Hakone or Kyoto but are genuinely excellent.

A final note on language. The staff will likely not speak much English, and that's genuinely fine. The experience requires very little language to work. 'Arigatou gozaimasu' (thank you very much) and 'oishii' (delicious) will handle 80% of situations. The rest comes from gestures, patience, and the fact that the people running a ryokan have been doing this for decades and know how to communicate without words.
heroImageAlt: A traditional ryokan room with tatami floor, low table, and garden view
author: TABI Editorial

title: Why January Is the Best Month to Visit Japan (Yes, Really)
excerpt: No cherry blossoms. No 38-degree humidity. No crowds. January is when Japan stops performing for tourists and just exists.
body: Summer in Japan is spectacular and exhausting. Cherry blossom season is magical and so crowded that the famous spots feel like theme parks. Autumn foliage is genuinely among the most beautiful things in the natural world and is also, by October, completely overrun. January has none of these problems, because January has a reputation for cold and grey that is only partly deserved.

The actual weather in Tokyo in January: between 4°C and 10°C during the day, clear skies more often than you'd expect, occasional rain, rare snow. Not harsh. A good wool coat is enough. Kyoto gets occasional snow — which on temple grounds with grey stones and winter moss looks exactly like the photograph you've seen your whole life, except there are almost no other people in it.

What January removes: the tourist surge disappears after New Year's (January 1–3), when domestic travelers return home and the holiday-season international visitors are gone. From January 5th onwards, you can visit Kinkaku-ji without being engulfed in 2,000 people. You can walk the Arashiyama bamboo grove with no one in your frame. This is not an exaggeration. On a cold January Tuesday, the path is genuinely empty.

What January adds: the rituals of New Year's — hatsumode, the first shrine visit of the year — give the first week of January a feeling of purposefulness that ordinary months don't have. Major shrines are packed January 1–3 and quieter but still festive through the first week. Asakusa's Senso-ji at 6am on January 2nd is one of the best crowds in the world to be part of.

Onsen towns are at their best in winter, and this is not a small thing. The specific pleasure of sitting in an outdoor rotenburo — a hot spring bath — with snow falling and steam rising is not available in July. Hakone from Tokyo, Kinosaki Onsen from Kyoto, Beppu in Kyushu: these are all better in winter on nearly every metric. The baths are hotter, the air is colder, and the bowl of food you eat afterwards is twice as satisfying.

Ski resorts: Japan has some of the best powder snow in the world. Hokkaido specifically — Niseko, Furano, Rusutsu — has developed into a serious international ski destination, partly because the light dry snow that falls there is genuinely exceptional. January is peak season. If skiing is relevant to your trip, January in Hokkaido is not a consolation prize. It's the main event.

The practical case: flights cost less in January than in October or April. Hotels cost less. Popular restaurants with months-long waiting lists have more availability. The ryokan in Hakone that has no rooms in November has rooms in January, often at a meaningfully lower rate.

What Japan offers in winter that it can't offer in spring or autumn: something closer to daily life. When a place is over-visited, it optimizes for visitors. When it's not being watched, it relaxes. January Japan is commuters and schoolchildren and the ramen shop that opens at 6:30am for the people who work early shifts. That Japan is worth seeing, and it's more accessible than people think.
heroImageAlt: Snow-dusted temple grounds in Kyoto in winter
author: TABI Editorial

title: The Convenience Store Is Japan's Greatest Culinary Achievement
excerpt: Seven-Eleven in Japan is not Seven-Eleven anywhere else. This distinction matters and deserves your full attention.
body: The first thing to understand about Japanese convenience stores — konbini — is that the food is genuinely good. Not 'good for a convenience store' good. Just good. The onigiri at 7-Eleven is made fresh every morning. The egg salad sandwich at Lawson is produced with a specific grade of bread and a specific ratio of egg to mayonnaise that has been optimized through decades of iteration. The hot foods at Family Mart are cooked to a standard that most fast-casual restaurants in other countries don't meet.

Konbini are everywhere. In Tokyo, you're never more than a three-minute walk from one. They're open 24 hours. They sell alcohol until midnight or all night depending on the prefecture. They have ATMs that accept foreign cards — the 7-Bank machines at 7-Eleven are the most consistently reliable. They sell stamps, ship packages, pay utility bills, and print documents from a USB drive. They are infrastructure, and understanding them is understanding how Japan works.

What to eat. Onigiri are rice balls with a filling — tuna mayo, pickled plum, salmon, bonito — wrapped in a three-panel system that keeps the seaweed crispy until you open it. The opening system is printed on the packet with numbered arrows. Follow them. Don't try to unwrap it another way. You will end up with rice everywhere and no dignified explanation.

The egg salad sandwich (tamago sando) at 7-Eleven specifically has developed a following that crosses national lines. The egg is thick-cut and slightly sweet. The bread is soft in the particular way that soft white bread in Japan is soft, which is different from anywhere else and difficult to explain. Buy one on your first morning and the question of why people travel to Japan specifically for the food will answer itself.

Hot food: the oden pot is a winter staple — simmering broth with fish cakes, tofu, daikon, and boiled eggs. You pick items with tongs and the staff counts them into your bill. Steamed buns (nikuman) live in the hot case near the register and are excellent at 11pm when nothing else is open. The Family Mart chicken has been the subject of genuine national debate about whether it surpasses McDonald's. The answer depends on the outlet and the day.

Drinks: canned coffee in Japan is a separate category of human achievement. Boss Coffee and the konbini own-brand cans have been developed through R&D cycles that most car companies would find excessive. Around ¥120 a can, available cold or hot depending on the season. The hot canned coffee on a November morning in Tokyo is one of those sensory memories that travel writers try to describe and never quite manage.

Practical notes: budget ¥500–700 for a full konbini meal. Pay at the register. Staff will ask 'atatame masuka?' (would you like this warmed up?) for eligible items. Yes is the answer. There will also be a question about a bag — a small fee applies, but it's worth it. Don't eat inside the store if it can be avoided; there's usually a counter or seats outside.
heroImageAlt: Onigiri and sandwiches at a Japanese convenience store counter
author: TABI Editorial

title: Tokyo's Kissaten: The Coffee Shops That Refuse to Change
excerpt: Somewhere between the third-wave cafés and the chain stores, Tokyo's old coffee shops have been quietly waiting.
body: The word kissaten has no clean English translation. A coffee shop, technically. But that misses the point. A kissaten is a room where time moves differently — where the owner has been making the same blend since 1978, where the cups are heavier than they need to be, where nobody is on a laptop, and where the ashtrays are still on the tables even if nobody uses them anymore.

Tokyo has thousands of them. Most are invisible to visitors because they don't appear on Instagram, they rarely have English menus, and the doors look like they open onto second-floor landings or basements with no visible signage. This is not an accident. Kissaten are not trying to attract new customers. They already have their customers, and those customers have been coming in for decades.

The ones worth finding are usually in older neighborhoods — Koenji, Shimokitazawa, Yanaka, the parts of Shinjuku that Airbnb hasn't reached yet. They cluster near train stations that aren't on the tourist circuit. The right strategy is to walk until you see a menu in the window that was laminated in approximately 1990.

What you're drinking is blend coffee — filter or drip coffee made with a proprietary blend the owner has been refining for decades. This is not specialty coffee in the third-wave sense. It's not trying to taste of blueberries or dried apricot. It tastes of coffee, deeply, without fuss. Served black by default, with a small jug of cream on the side.

Food at a kissaten tends to be one of two things: a morning set or toast. The morning set, usually available until 11am, is a cup of coffee plus a thick slice of toast with butter and jam — sometimes with a small boiled egg on the side — for ¥400 to ¥600. It is one of the great underrated breakfasts in the world, and it is not something you will find on any list of Tokyo's best breakfasts.

Sitting in a kissaten requires a different pace. You don't order at a counter. You sit down, wait for the owner to come to you, and order quietly. Then you wait. The coffee takes longer to arrive than you'd expect, and you will feel, briefly, uncomfortable with the silence. That feeling passes. That's the point.

A few worth knowing in Tokyo: Café de l'Ambre in Ginza (open since 1948; the owner worked there into his nineties), Chatei Hatou in Shibuya (precise and serious about the coffee), Kayaba Coffee in Yanaka (technically new, but in a building from 1938 that does the work). Don't go to all of them. Pick one, sit down properly, and stay for at least an hour.

The reason kissaten deserve attention isn't nostalgia, exactly. It's that they represent a way of organizing time that has largely been optimized away. Nobody is tracking you. Nobody cares about table turnover. You ordered coffee and you're welcome to stay all morning. That this now feels unusual is its own kind of commentary.
heroImageAlt: A dimly lit kissaten coffee shop interior with wooden counters and vintage cups
author: TABI Editorial

title: Kanazawa: The City Most Japan Itineraries Skip — and Shouldn't
excerpt: Three hours from Tokyo by bullet train. A fraction of Kyoto's crowds. Arguably better seafood. There is no good reason it's on so few itineraries.
body: Kanazawa is on the wrong side of Japan. That seems to be the only explanation for why it receives a third of Kyoto's visitor numbers while having a castle, a geisha district, one of Japan's three great landscape gardens, and what most serious food people consider the best seafood market outside of Tokyo.

The bullet train from Tokyo (the Hokuriku Shinkansen, extended in 2015) takes 2.5 hours. From Kyoto it's 2.5 to 3 hours with a transfer. Neither journey is difficult. The problem is psychological — most visitors arrive with a Tokyo-Kyoto-Osaka triangle already built into their itinerary, and Kanazawa sits outside of it, requiring a detour that most itineraries can't absorb.

Kenroku-en is the main draw, and correctly so. One of Japan's three great gardens — a designation given by Edo-period landscape critics — it was developed over two centuries by the ruling Maeda clan and covers 11.4 hectares. In cherry blossom season it's crowded. In late November, when the pine trees are wrapped in rope against the winter snow in a technique called yukizuri, it's extraordinary and very nearly empty.

Higashi Chaya is Kanazawa's best-preserved geisha quarter and is smaller and less tourist-managed than Kyoto's Gion. Some of the ochaya (teahouses) are still operating. The street is wooden and narrow and hasn't changed structurally since the early 19th century. Come early morning or at dusk and it's yours.

The food. Kanazawa sits on the Sea of Japan coast and receives fish from waters that don't reach the Pacific side in the same condition. The seafood at Omicho Market — a covered market the size of a small neighborhood — is some of the best in the country. In winter: crab, specifically the male snow crab called zuwaigani. In spring: firefly squid. Year-round: a sashimi set from any mid-range restaurant near the market will be better than most of what you'd eat in a Tokyo tourist district at twice the price.

Kanazawa also has a serious craft tradition. Gold leaf production has been centered here since the Edo period — 99% of Japan's kinpaku (gold leaf) still comes from Kanazawa workshops. The '21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art' (designed by SANAA, open since 2004) is one of the better contemporary art museums in Japan and draws a local crowd, not just tourists.

Where to stay: the city center is small enough that almost anywhere is walkable. The area around Katamachi is convenient and has good restaurant density. Stay two nights minimum. The city rewards an unhurried second day in a way that a single overnight doesn't capture.

What Kanazawa has that Kyoto has largely lost is the feeling of visiting somewhere people actually live. The geisha quarter isn't a theme park. The old town is genuinely old and genuinely in use. If you're looking for that — and increasingly, it's what travelers say they want — Kanazawa is close to the correct answer.
heroImageAlt: Higashi Chaya geisha district in Kanazawa, wooden ochaya buildings lining a quiet street
author: TABI Editorial

title: Tsukiji Outer Market: Where to Actually Eat (and What to Skip)
excerpt: The inner market moved to Toyosu in 2018. The outer market never moved and never needed to.
body: In 2018, the famous wholesale fish market at Tsukiji relocated operations to Toyosu, a reclaimed island further east. Travel writers immediately started writing elegies for Tsukiji. What they missed is that the part most visitors cared about — the outer market, with its 400-odd shops selling fresh seafood, street food, knives, and tamago-yaki — never went anywhere and shows no signs of going anywhere.

The outer market opens early. Most stalls are running by 6am. The target arrival window is between 7am and 10am, when the selection is widest and the food is freshest. By noon, the best stalls will have sold out of the things worth eating. Come for breakfast. This is not negotiable.

What to eat, in order of importance. First: sushi from one of the small counters in the side streets off the main market lane. These are eight-seat affairs with a glass case of fish in front. Point at what you want, sit down, eat it immediately. The fish was at Toyosu wholesale market this morning. A ¥1,500–2,000 set will be the best sushi you've had in your life, and if you don't believe that until you're eating it, that's fine.

Second: tamagoyaki, the thick-rolled sweet omelette that is specific to the Tsukiji style. The most famous vendors are Tamagoyaki-ya Shouro and the stalls along the Namiyoke Shrine side. They sell slices from the pan on wooden sticks. The outside is slightly caramelized. The inside is custard-soft. It costs around ¥200 and you will want two.

Third: ikura (salmon roe) on rice, from any stall that makes it fresh. The roe should be bright orange and very fresh — the texture is different from what you've likely had before. A small bowl is ¥500–800. It's one of those things that recalibrates your understanding of what fish can be.

What to skip: the photographed seafood-on-skewer vendors near the main entrance that have been optimized for social media. The scallops are fine but priced for tourists, and the line is full of people taking the same photo. Walk past them, turn into the side streets, and find the stalls where nobody has their phone out.

The knife shops deserve a separate mention. Tsukiji has more professional knife dealers per square meter than anywhere outside of the knife-making cities. If you're planning to buy Japanese kitchen knives — and you should, they are among the best things to bring home from Japan — buy them here after handling them and talking to the vendor. The selection goes from ¥3,000 santoku knives for everyday home cooking to ¥80,000 custom yanagiba for the serious cook.
Getting there: subway to Tsukijishijo (Oedo Line) or Tsukiji (Hibiya Line). Allow at least two hours. Three if you're serious about the knives.
heroImageAlt: Fresh seafood stalls at Tsukiji outer market at early morning
author: TABI Editorial

title: How to Eat at an Izakaya Without Looking Completely Lost
excerpt: The curtain over the door. The laminated menu with pictures on maybe two pages. The small charge you didn't order. You'll be fine.
body: An izakaya is a Japanese pub — not a restaurant, not a bar, but the thing that happens when the two concepts are combined without either one winning. You drink and eat in roughly equal measure. The food comes in small plates meant to be shared across the table. Nobody orders a main course. The lighting is low and the room is loud and you'll probably be sharing it with salarymen who have loosened their ties, which is always a good sign.

Most izakaya have a printed menu and sometimes a laminated picture menu. Order from the picture menu if there is one. Point at what you want. Nobody will be offended. If there's no picture menu, say 'osusume wa nan desu ka?' — what do you recommend? Someone will point at something, and whatever they point at will be edible.

The standard opening order: a round of beers (nama biru, draft, or a sawa cocktail made from shochu and soda), plus two or three small dishes to start. Edamame, karaage (fried chicken), and potato salad are almost universally available and almost universally good. They are not exciting. They are correct. Order them while you figure out the rest of the menu.

The small charge that appears on your bill without explanation: it's called otoshi. Almost every izakaya charges a per-person seating fee of ¥200–600, and in return brings a small snack you didn't order — sometimes pickled vegetables, sometimes a small salad, occasionally something genuinely excellent. It's not a scam. It's standard. The bill is correct.

Order drinks first, then food, then more drinks, then more food — loosely. Good things to order beyond the basics: yakitori (grilled chicken on bamboo skewers — order mixed, specify 'tare' for sweet soy glaze or 'shio' for salt), dashimaki tamago (rolled omelette made with dashi broth, usually soft and fragrant), and sashimi if the place looks like it takes its fish seriously, which you can usually tell from whether there are locals at the bar eating it.

Shochu is likely what you're drinking in the cocktails and don't know it. Distilled most commonly from sweet potato (imo-jochu) or barley (mugi-jochu), it's served over ice or mixed with soda. Gentler than whisky, it goes with everything on the menu. If someone offers chuhai, that's shochu with fruit soda — the izakaya equivalent of a spritz, and perfectly fine.

You leave when you've decided to leave. To get the bill, catch someone's eye and make a rectangular gesture with your index fingers — this is the universal 'o-kaikei' signal and everyone will understand it immediately. The total will likely be split evenly if you're with a group. This is called warikan. Don't try to calculate individual shares. It goes against the spirit of the thing.

The golden rule: the more local and less translated the menu, the better the food. The izakaya in a residential neighborhood near a train station that isn't on any tourist map will beat the one with an English sign near the temples every time. Your hotel is on the tourist circuit. The izakaya should not be.
heroImageAlt: Inside a Tokyo izakaya with warm lighting, wooden tables and yakitori on the grill
author: TABI Editorial

title: The IC Card Is the Best Thing in Japan. Here's How to Actually Use It.
excerpt: You can survive Tokyo's train network without one. You just won't want to.
body: Before you leave the airport, find a ticket machine and get a Suica. Don't deliberate. Don't check whether you really need one. You need one.

An IC card is a rechargeable transit card — the same basic technology as London's Oyster or Hong Kong's Octopus — except that in Japan it works on practically everything. Trains, subways, buses, ferries, taxis in major cities, convenience stores, vending machines, coin lockers, the self-checkout at Don Quijote at 2am. The list keeps growing.

Suica is issued by JR East and works in Tokyo and most of eastern Japan. ICOCA is the Kansai equivalent, issued by JR West, and works in Osaka, Kyoto, Kobe, and surrounding areas. The practical answer to which one you should get: they're fully interoperable. Get whichever one is available at the airport you land at. It genuinely doesn't matter.

Loading money: find any green JR ticket machine, insert your card, press 'Charge' (there's an English option), and add ¥1,000, ¥2,000, or ¥5,000. Keep ¥2,000 or more on the card at all times. You don't want to be the person topping up at the fare gate while a queue builds behind you.

Tapping is the ritual that will define your first week. Tap the card on the reader as you enter the gate — a satisfying beep. Tap again as you leave — another beep, money deducted. A sad double-beep and a red light means insufficient balance. Step aside, top up at the nearest machine, which will be approximately five meters away.

One thing nobody mentions: the card works in vending machines too. Just tap the reader on the front panel. It's faster than coins, works every time, and makes carrying change feel archaic. After a week in Japan you'll find physical coins faintly offensive.

Increasingly, you can also link Suica to Apple Pay or Google Pay. Setup takes about ten minutes and is worth doing if your phone supports it — you can top up from the app, which means you never need to find a machine. The setup process is in English on both platforms.

When you leave Japan: keep the card. It doesn't expire, and having it ready for your next trip is genuinely convenient. If you want to return it, do so at a JR ticket office — you'll get back your remaining balance minus a ¥220 handling fee. If you have less than ¥300 left, just keep it.

The larger point is this: the IC card makes it possible to navigate Tokyo's 130-plus stations within the Yamanote Line loop without ever buying a specific-destination ticket from a machine. That is not a trivial thing. It removes a class of friction that makes many transit systems — especially large Japanese ones — intimidating for first-time visitors. Get the card at the airport. Everything else is easier.
heroImageAlt: Commuter tapping a Suica IC card at a Tokyo train station gate
author: TABI Editorial

title: Japanese Whisky: What's Worth Buying and What's Just Marketing
excerpt: The golden age is over but the whisky is still exceptional. You just need to know what you're actually looking at.
body: Somewhere around 2014, the world decided Japanese whisky was the best in the world. Suntory's Yamazaki 12 Year won a prominent international award. Whisky writers made pilgrimages to Nikka's distillery in Hokkaido. By 2016 you couldn't find a bottle of Yamazaki Single Malt for under ¥15,000 in a Tokyo duty-free. By 2020 you couldn't find it at all.

The scarcity problem is real but overstated. What happened: global demand exploded, Japanese distilleries — which had been producing at a domestic-consumption pace — couldn't scale fast enough. The aged expressions ran dry. Secondary market prices went insane. But the distilleries kept working, and the whisky that was aging in 2016 and 2018 is now maturing. Availability is slowly improving.

What's genuinely worth buying in 2026. Nikka From The Barrel: a blended whisky that consistently outperforms its price. Around ¥3,000 at a convenience store if you're lucky enough to find it. Suntory Toki: an affordable blend designed for highballs — order it at a bar before you commit to a bottle, which will take about one sip. Any single malt expression from Yamazaki or Yoichi that you can find at close to retail price rather than secondary market markup.

The highball is the format. Japanese whisky culture is built around the whisky highball — spirit over ice with soda, served in a tall frosted glass. It sounds humble. It isn't. Order one at any decent whisky bar before you start shopping; it will calibrate your sense of what you're buying.

Where to buy: department store basement floors (depachika) and specialist liquor stores have the best selection and prices. Airport duty-free is convenient but the offering has narrowed significantly since the shortage began. Avoid hotel gift shops. The markup is severe and the selection is whatever moves fastest with tourists.

On labeling: 'Japanese whisky' now has legal standards, introduced in 2021. The spirit must be made in Japan from Japanese ingredients, malted and aged in Japan, and bottled in Japan. Before this regulation, some brands were blending imported Scottish or Canadian spirit and calling it Japanese. The rules now require transparency. If a bottle says 'blended' without a specific distillery name, read the fine print.

What you're most likely to actually find and afford: Suntory Kakubin (yellow label, a classic blend, excellent for highballs, around ¥1,200 at most liquor shops), Nikka Coffey Malt or Coffey Grain (made in a Coffey still, unusual and worth the ¥7,000), and possibly Chichibu expressions from Ichiro's Malt if you're prepared to spend ¥15,000 or more on something genuinely scarce.

One bottle to look for specifically: Mars Komagatake, from the highest-altitude distillery in Japan, located in Nagano. Production is limited and domestic allocation is small, but it shows up in specialist shops and occasionally at airport duty-free. It's not as famous as Yamazaki, but the gap in quality doesn't justify the gap in price and availability. Find it while that's still true.
heroImageAlt: Japanese whisky bottles displayed at a specialist liquor shop in Tokyo
author: TABI Editorial

title: Naoshima Is Japan's Art Island — and Nobody Tells You How to Do It Right
excerpt: It's a real island with real people living on it. The art just happens to be everywhere.
body: Naoshima sits in the Seto Inland Sea between Okayama and Takamatsu, about an hour by ferry from either city. It's small enough to cross on a rented bicycle in an afternoon. The population is around 3,000. The island has more Monets per square kilometer than most museums in Europe, which is a fact that sounds invented but isn't.

The backstory: in the 1980s and 90s, the Benesse Corporation commissioned architect Tadao Ando to build a museum on a largely depopulated island. What followed over the next three decades was a collaboration between art, architecture, and landscape that turned Naoshima into something that shouldn't quite work but absolutely does. It's been widely imitated. Nothing else is quite like it.

The main venues. The Chichu Art Museum is underground — no exterior visible from above, all natural light coming through skylights — and contains exactly three artists: Monet (five late Water Lilies), James Turrell (light installations), and Walter De Maria. That's all. The ticket is ¥2,100 and it is worth the trip from Tokyo to stand in the Monet room, which is a thing I don't say lightly.

The Art House Project is what separates Naoshima from every other art destination in Japan. Artists were given abandoned houses in Honmura village and told to do what they wanted. James Turrell built a light installation into a farmhouse that you enter barefoot and lie on the floor of. Hiroshi Sugimoto turned a shrine into something else. These aren't galleries with labels and benches. They're encounters.

Getting there from Tokyo is a half-day commitment minimum. Bullet train to Okayama, 20 minutes by local train to Uno port, 20-minute ferry to Naoshima. From Takamatsu it's about an hour by ferry. The journey filters out casual visitors, which is exactly why the island still has the quality it has.

Stay overnight if you can. After the day-trip visitors leave on the last ferry, the island changes. The Art House Project buildings glow from inside. The grounds around Benesse House are open and quiet. You'll be one of a small number of people who aren't locals. This is an unusual experience in Japan and should be used properly — walk around, eat at the small restaurant near the port, sit somewhere without your phone.

On bicycles: the island is hilly and summer heat is serious. Electric bikes are available at the ferry terminal and are not cheating. The climb between Honmura and the Benesse area in August on a regular bicycle is not a good time.

Naoshima rewards a particular mindset: willingness to be disoriented, to find things with no label, to sit with something that doesn't explain itself. If that's what you came to Japan for — and increasingly, it is — the island earns its reputation. If you're looking for informational plaques and a gift shop at every turn, the Benesse Foundation apologizes in advance.
heroImageAlt: The yellow pumpkin sculpture by Yayoi Kusama on the coast of Naoshima
author: TABI Editorial

title: The Hidden Shrines of Kyoto Locals Still Keep Secret
summary: Beyond Fushimi Inari and Kinkaku-ji lies another Kyoto — quiet, moss-covered, and entirely yours. We asked local monks and longtime residents where they actually go when the crowds become too much. These are the shrines that rarely appear in guidebooks.

title: How to Spend 10 Days in Japan Without a Single Tourist Trap
summary: Most first-time visitors follow the same Golden Route — Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka — and come home feeling like they saw a theme park. This itinerary takes a different approach: slower, deeper, and built around the neighborhoods that locals actually use.

title: The Perfect Route up Mt. Fuji: What Nobody Tells You
summary: Climbing Fuji is one of those experiences that sounds straightforward until you're doing it at 3am in a headlamp queue. The mountain rewards preparation and punishes assumptions — here's what we learned from five ascents across different seasons.

title: Osaka in 3 Days: The Eating and Wandering Itinerary
summary: Osaka has a reputation as Japan's kitchen, but that barely scratches the surface. This city rewards those who eat their way through it without a plan — we've structured the chaos into three days that still feel spontaneous.

title: Yakushima: Japan's Ancient Forest That Few Tourists Find
summary: A UNESCO-listed island covered in cedar trees that are older than many civilizations, Yakushima sits off the southern tip of Kyushu and sees a fraction of the visitors it deserves. The hike through Shiratani Unsuikyo inspired Princess Mononoke — and it still feels that way.

title: How to Read a Japanese Menu Without Speaking Japanese
summary: Most Japanese restaurants don't have English menus, and that's not a problem once you know what to look for. A handful of kanji, some pattern recognition, and the willingness to point gets you through almost any restaurant in the country.

title: What Wabi-Sabi Really Means — And Why the World Needs It Now
summary: The Japanese aesthetic of imperfection and transience offers something Western design philosophy rarely does: permission to be incomplete. Wabi-sabi isn't a style — it's a way of perceiving, and learning to see it changes how you look at almost everything.

title: Kintsugi: The Art of Repairing Broken Things with Gold
summary: A 500-year-old technique for mending ceramics with lacquer mixed with gold, silver, or platinum has become a global metaphor for resilience. We visited a master craftsman in Kyoto to understand why kintsugi is about far more than broken bowls.

title: Hanami 2026: Where to See Cherry Blossoms Like a Local
summary: Skip the tourist crowds at Maruyama Park and Ueno. The best hanami spots are the ones you stumble into — a neighborhood embankment, a school gate, a canal nobody bothered to list. We've found them, so you don't have to.

title: The Best Japanese Kitchen Knives to Bring Home
summary: Japanese knives are the single best souvenir you can carry out of Japan — functional, beautiful, and something you'll use every day for decades. This guide covers every price point from a ¥4,000 entry knife to the hand-forged blade worth saving up for.

title: Buying Matcha: Ceremonial vs. Culinary Grade Explained
summary: The matcha market is flooded with products that taste nothing like what you drink in a tea ceremony — and the price tag is not a reliable guide. Here's how to read labels, what to taste for, and where to buy the real thing.

title: 10 Japanese Drugstore Products Worth Every Yen
summary: Japanese pharmacies are overwhelming in the best way — walls of skincare, hair treatments, and stomach remedies at prices that make no sense until you try them. These are the ten products that actually earn their place in your luggage.

title: Where to Buy Authentic Yukata in Tokyo Without the Markup
summary: The tourist-district yukata sold near Asakusa are fine, but the ones sold at department store basements and specialist shops are extraordinary — and often cheaper. This guide takes you past the obvious options to the places worth visiting.
